description: Figure 13 presents a comparative analysis of TiB₂ particle damage evolution in a low-density steel (Fe–15.5Mn–3.95Al–5Ti–2.15B) under tensile loading at 5 % and 7 % global engineering strain, coupling scanning electron microscopy (SEM), micro-digital image correlation (μ-DIC), and finite element (FE) simulations. At 5 % strain, subfigures (a)–(a₃) show experimental SEM images of Particle 1 and Particle 2 fracture, alongside corresponding equivalent strain maps from μ-DIC (a₁), plastic equivalent strain (PEEQ) maps from FE (a₂), and σ₁₁ stress histories versus global strain (a₃). At 7 % strain, subfigures (b)–(b₃) capture Particle 3 decohesion and Particle 4 fracture, with matching strain distributions (b₁), PEEQ distributions (b₂), and stress curves revealing debonding and two-stage fracture events (b₃). Subfigure (c) schematically summarizes the microstructure evolution, depicting TiB₂ particle cracking voids and interfacial debonding voids within the δ-ferrite and austenite matrix. The figure demonstrates that localized strain concentrations drive both particle fracture and interfacial decohesion, with agglomerated particles fracturing preferentially due to initial defects, and confirms that particle fracture accelerates final specimen failure more severely than decohesion, validating the FE model's predictive capability.
